title: On Local Systems of Geometric Origin
speaker: Alexander Petrov
abstract: I will discuss the following conjecture: an irreducible Q¯ ℓ-local system L on a smooth complex algebraic variety S arises in cohomology of a family of varieties over S if and only if L can be extended to an etale local system over some descent of S to a finitely-generated subfield of complex numbers.  I will describe the motivation for this conjecture coming from relative p-adic Hodge theory, known partial results, and possible approaches (not very successful so far) to formulating a purely p-adic (and thus hopefully more tractable) version of this conjecture.

proof_stages.label: Definitions of geometric origin and arithmetic descent
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes a smooth complex algebraic variety and defines a Qpbar local system to be of geometric origin if it occurs as a subquotient of relative homology over a dense open, and arithmetic if it descends to a model over a finitely generated field.

proof_stages.label: Main equivalence conjecture and elementary evidence
proof_stages.summary: The central conjecture is stated: for irreducible local systems, arithmeticity should be equivalent to geometric origin; the known direction from geometry to arithmetic, the rank-one case, and uniqueness up to Galois-character twist are explained.

proof_stages.label: Finite Galois orbit reformulation
proof_stages.summary: After restricting to integral p-adic representations, arithmeticity is rephrased as having finite orbit under the Galois action on the representation variety, making the conjecture parallel to the Tate conjecture for finite-orbit cohomology classes.

proof_stages.label: Relative Fontaine–Mazur conjectural input
proof_stages.summary: A relative Fontaine–Mazur conjecture is stated for irreducible p-adic local systems on varieties over number fields: after a cyclotomic twist, geometric origin should be equivalent to almost-everywhere unramifiedness plus de Rham behavior at p-adic places.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to a p-adic twisting theorem
proof_stages.summary: The almost-everywhere unramified condition is shown to be automatic for geometrically irreducible local systems via Galois actions on prime-to-bad pro-completions of the geometric fundamental group, and the de Rham condition is reduced to a purely p-adic theorem saying such local systems can be twisted by a character to become de Rham.

proof_stages.label: Decompleted p-adic Riemann–Hilbert functor
proof_stages.summary: The proof sketch introduces a decompleted p-adic Riemann–Hilbert/Simpson functor RH+ from p-adic local systems to vector bundles on a formal t-thickening with a flat connection, including the t-direction, and identifies de Rhamness with preservation of rank after taking t-flat sections.

proof_stages.label: Full-faithfulness substitute and twisting proof
proof_stages.summary: A modified Hom comparison and a linear-algebra classification of RH+ objects with scalar endomorphisms force RH+(L) to have a single logarithmic t-residue, and twisting by a character with the opposite Hodge–Tate weight removes this residue and makes the local system de Rham.

proof_stages.label: Finite-field evidence and p-adic open problem
proof_stages.summary: As further evidence and warning, the speaker discusses the finite-field curve theorem of Drinfeld–Lafforgue, the failure of naive one-prime specialization due to a sharp finite-field/complex discrepancy, and ends by asking for a purely p-adic condition stronger than crystallinity that would force geometric origin.

examples.name: Rank-one local systems from finite covers
examples.note: Rank-one arithmetic local systems are finite-order characters, hence are killed by a finite étale cover and occur in degree-zero homology, illustrating the backward direction in the simplest case.
examples.engagement_reason: He proves the rank-one case by identifying arithmetic rank-one systems with finite-order characters, passing to the finite étale cover that trivializes them, and realizing them in H_0 of that cover.

examples.name: Tate conjecture analogy
examples.note: The finite-Galois-orbit condition for representations is compared with the Tate conjecture, where finite-orbit cohomology classes should be spanned by algebraic cycles.
examples.engagement_reason: He states the Tate conjecture as an analogy—finite Galois orbits in cohomology spanned by cycles—but does not compute or apply it to a concrete case.

examples.name: Point case of relative Fontaine–Mazur
examples.note: An audience question notes that taking the base variety to be a point recovers the classical Fontaine–Mazur-type situation for Galois representations of a number field.
examples.engagement_reason: He specializes the relative Fontaine–Mazur setup to the point case, notes that geometric irreducibility then forces rank one, and later computes the RH+ object and flat sections for cyclotomic characters over a point.

examples.name: Curves over finite fields
examples.note: The Drinfeld–Lafforgue theorem for curves over finite fields gives an analogue in which irreducible local systems of finite-field descent are of geometric origin, serving as evidence for the characteristic-zero conjecture.
examples.engagement_reason: He cites the Drinfeld–Lafforgue theorem for curves over finite fields and uses it as evidence, without working through a particular curve or construction.

examples.name: Finite-field versus complex rank-two discrepancy
examples.note: The contrast between infinitely many geometric rank-two local systems on curves over algebraic closures of finite fields and none on most complex genus-two curves forces the idea that a characteristic-zero proof cannot use a single specialization prime naively.
examples.engagement_reason: He analyzes the rank-two discrepancy by comparing the abundance of finite-field geometric local systems with the scarcity over complex genus-two curves and draws consequences for possible proof strategies.

passes.reason: The rank-two finite-field/complex discrepancy is used to show that a naive one-prime specialization argument cannot work.
The rank-two finite-field/complex discrepancy is used as an obstruction to a naive one-prime transfer argument.

examples.name: Crystalline but non-geometric local systems
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker cites constructions of many crystalline local systems together with scarcity of geometric-origin systems, showing that crystallinity alone is insufficient and motivating an additional p-adic condition.
examples.engagement_reason: He only reports that examples of crystalline but non-geometric local systems exist via Clark’s scarcity result and another construction, without describing or checking one.
